cheetochopsticks · civic audit memo · no. 12-C (consolidated)

Seven Charts. One Broken Pipe.

A consolidated audit of every public chart, map, and story view built on the CSPD Crash Data dataset. Every one of them inherits the same upstream data problems, plus each adds its own visual artifact that misleads the audiences it was built to serve. This memo also documents two site-wide accessibility failures that apply to every page on the portal.

Scope
policedata.coloradosprings.gov — Traffic Crashes category
Views audited
7 chart/map views + 1 story page + underlying dataset
Underlying data
bjpt-tkzq (Crash Data for At Fault Drivers) · 95,200 rows
Platform
Tyler Data & Insights (formerly Socrata)
Memo date
April 17, 2026
Relationship
Follows 12-A audit, 12-B register, and intro email already sent April 17
Precedes
Follow-up email (planned, post-April 24 if needed)

ISummary

  1. Every chart view on the portal is a derived view of bjpt-tkzq. Seven public visualizations (plus one narrative story page) all pull from the same underlying dataset. There is no independent validation layer between that dataset and the charts the public sees. Every finding from the April 17 parent audit propagates through every chart, uncorrected and unflagged.
  2. Each chart adds its own visual artifact. The injury-over-time chart shows a 91% injury-rate jump that is actually a classification shift. The by-cause charts show "NO APPARENT FACTOR" as the dominant traffic-safety explanation. The fatalities-by-cause chart shows DUI at one-fifteenth of the state baseline. The neighborhood map has 34 fatal crashes (7.4%) invisible because of missing geocoding — and for 2025 specifically, one in seven crashes is missing from the map.
  3. Two accessibility failures apply to every page. The site-wide footer Accessibility link points to a dead vendor domain. Every dataset, chart, and story page renders content client-side only, so screen readers, search engines, and archival tools see an empty shell.
  4. The comparable state-level dashboard carries an explicit disclaimer. CDOT publishes a standing notice on every crash page: "Colorado traffic crashes as presented in the dashboard should be regarded as preliminary and subject to change." CSPD's dashboards carry no equivalent caveat.
  5. The fix for most findings is configuration, not code. The accessibility link, range constraints on numeric fields, dataset-level disclaimer text, and chart annotations are all settings in the Tyler admin panel. A staff administrator can close roughly half of this memo in one working day.

Contents

  1. IIThe Chart Portfolio
  2. IIISite-Wide Findings
  3. IVPer-View Findings
  4. VCross-View Matrix
  5. VIRecommendations
  6. VIIPeer Comparison

IIThe Chart Portfolio

The following views were identified from the portal's catalog and confirmed via search indexing. Every one of them is tagged as a derived view of "Crash Data for At Fault Drivers" (dataset bjpt-tkzq). None of them exposes a data-quality statement, a last-validated date, or a link to the underlying issues documented in the parent audit.

Public views on policedata.coloradosprings.gov — Traffic Crashes category
IDTitleTypeRole
bjpt-tkzqCrash DataTabular datasetThe source. 95,200 rows. Parent audit covers this.
sfgg-z2d5Accidents Involving Injuries Over TimeTime-series chartShows injury counts climbing. Memo 12-B covers this.
qy5n-5p2nTraffic Fatalities by CausesCategorical chartBreakdown of fatality causes.
7xij-tg78Traffic Crashes by CausesCategorical chartBreakdown of all-crash causes.
rt4j-ce7kCrash Data by Date with AttributesTime-series, filterableChart with multi-attribute overlay.
j9ru-uz6wNumber of Crashes by Neighborhood MapChoropleth mapSpatial distribution of crashes.
mxkg-snghTraffic Citation Issued in Crash GraphCategorical chartCitation-issued yes/no distribution.
58nk-wiu5Crashes in Colorado SpringsStory pageNarrative aggregating several views.

IIISite-Wide Findings

These findings apply to every page on the portal — the dataset table, every chart view, the story page, the "About" page, and the home page. They are not per-view issues.

Critical · SW-1 — The accessibility link resolves to nothing

Every page's footer links "Accessibility" to http://www.socrata.com/accessibility — HTTP not HTTPS, a defunct vendor brand since Tyler's 2018 acquisition, and the URL returns empty content when fetched. Under 28 C.F.R. § 35.105, ADA Title II requires an accessible mechanism for the public to report accessibility barriers. A broken link does not meet this. Fix: template edit, fifteen minutes, city IT.

Critical · SW-2 — No server-rendered content anywhere

Every view URL (dataset, chart, map, story) returns the identical Tyler application shell when fetched without JavaScript execution. The chart titles change, but the actual visualizations, data, filter controls, legends, and descriptions exist only after client-side code runs. Consequences:

  • Assistive technology. Older screen readers, or those behind strict content-security policies, may receive only "Skip to main content" and the footer.
  • Search indexing. The content is effectively invisible to traditional crawlers. Public interest data that does not appear in search is difficult for the public to discover.
  • Archival. Tools like the Internet Archive Wayback Machine capture the shell but not the data. A journalist saving a chart as evidence gets a screenshot, not a reusable record.
  • Low-bandwidth access. Users on throttled connections, older devices, or enterprise networks that block heavy SPAs cannot see the content at all.

Fix: this is vendor-level and requires Tyler to add a server-rendered fallback, or the city to publish mirror pages with static summaries. The city cannot repair this alone, but it can publish its own fallback.

Warning · SW-3 — No data-quality disclaimer on the portal or any view

The Colorado Department of Transportation, which publishes the state-level crash dashboard, prepends this disclaimer to every page: "Colorado traffic crashes as presented in the dashboard should be regarded as preliminary and subject to change." CSPD's portal carries no equivalent — not on individual views, not portal-wide, not linked from the "About" story. The absence is a governance gap the city can close with one text block in the dataset description field.

IVPer-View Findings

Each card below states what the view shows, what is wrong, and how bad. Severity grades: Critical (materially misleads a reasonable user), Warning (missing caveat or partial-accuracy concern), Note (observation for future remediation).

Accidents Involving Injuries Over Time

sfgg-z2d5 · covered in memo 12-B

Time-series chart of injury crashes. Inherits the May 2021 pedestrian/bicycle cutoff. Produces a 91% injury-rate jump that is a classification change, not a public safety trend. See memo 12-B for full treatment.

Critical Classification artifact presented as safety trend

Traffic Fatalities by Causes

qy5n-5p2n

This chart groups the 457 fatal crashes in the dataset by their Driver Contributing Factor. The public audience for this view is anyone asking "why are people dying on Colorado Springs roads?" What the chart answers, at the actual field values:

Live distribution for qy5n-5p2n — all values that appear in the data
Cause categoryFatal crashes% of total
NO APPARENT FACTOR27560.2%
(null / not coded)8919.5%
OTHER FACTOR408.8%
DISTRACTED BY OTHER122.6%
DUI, DWAI, DUID92.0%
DRIVER INEXPERIENCE81.8%
AGGRESSIVE DRIVING61.3%
DISTRACTED BY CELL PHONE51.1%
DRIVER EMOTIONALLY UPSET40.9%
DRIVER UNFAMILIAR WITH AREA40.9%
DISTRACTED BY PASSENGER30.7%
EVADING LAW ENFORCEMENT OFFICER10.2%
ASLEEP AT THE WHEEL10.2%

Critical · QY-1 — DUI coded at one-fifteenth of state baseline

Colorado's state DUI fatality share per NHTSA/CDOT is 30–35%. This chart displays 2.0%. Applied to 457 fatal crashes, the state baseline would predict 135–160 DUI-involved rows. The dataset records nine.

Critical · QY-2 — "NO APPARENT FACTOR" as headline cause of death

Sixty percent of all fatal-crash rows are coded with a category name that tells the public nothing. Combined with the 19.5% null rate, 80% of the displayed fatal-cause chart is non-informative. A member of the public reading this chart cannot learn from it what causes traffic deaths in their city.

Critical Critical

Traffic Crashes by Causes

7xij-tg78

Same breakdown as the fatalities chart but applied to all 95,200 crashes. The same problems compound at larger scale.

Live distribution for 7xij-tg78 — top 6 categories
Cause categoryCrashes% of total
NO APPARENT FACTOR54,93257.7%
(null / not coded)20,99422.1%
OTHER FACTOR7,5988.0%
DUI, DWAI, DUID2,9033.0%
DRIVER INEXPERIENCE2,4682.6%
DISTRACTED BY OTHER2,0902.2%

Critical · 7X-1 — 80% of the chart is uncoded

Of 95,200 crashes, 75,926 (79.8%) are coded as either "NO APPARENT FACTOR" or null. The chart's headline visual is a pie or bar chart where roughly four out of five slices are non-informative. This is not a data distribution; it is a reporting failure. A coding audit upstream would reduce this materially; until it happens, the chart should carry a banner stating that most rows lack a coded cause.

Critical

Crash Data by Date with Attributes

rt4j-ce7k · last updated March 2 2026

A filterable time-series with overlaid attribute series. Plotting any of the pedestrian, bicycle, injury, or location-complete series over time will draw attention to the same cutoff points and pipeline failures as the per-view charts — but without any of the discovered context.

Warning · RT-1 — The filter UI makes discovery of zero-lines deceptively easy

Because this chart lets the public toggle attributes on and off, a user is substantially more likely to notice the pedestrian and bicycle lines going to zero than they are to notice it on a single fixed chart. This is good for data transparency if contextualized. It is bad for public understanding if uncaptioned. A citizen discovering "pedestrian crashes went to zero in mid-2021" has no way to learn that the underlying field stopped being populated rather than the underlying reality stopped happening.

Warning

Number of Crashes by Neighborhood Map

j9ru-uz6w

Choropleth map plotting crash counts against Colorado Springs neighborhood polygons via a computed-region join. Because the map depends on each row having a valid Location Point, any crash without geocoding is simply absent from the map — silently.

Crashes missing Location Point — share not appearing on the map
PopulationTotalMissing location% invisible
All crashes, 10 years95,2003,6553.8%
Fatal crashes, 10 years457347.4%
2025 crashes only8,3291,00412.1%
2025 July peak75915620.6%

Critical · JN-1 — 2025 geolocation failure

The missing-location rate was 2–4% every month from 2016 through December 2024, held steady through 2020's COVID disruption, and jumped to 7–20% starting January 2025. The July 2025 peak of 20.6% means one in five crashes that month did not show up on the public neighborhood map. The rate recovered briefly in November 2025 and has been climbing again in 2026. Something changed in the geocoding pipeline in late 2024 or early 2025. The chart is silently missing hundreds of crash locations for the most recent year.

Warning · JN-2 — Fatal crashes over-represented in missing locations

Fatal crashes are almost twice as likely as crashes overall to lack a Location Point (7.4% vs 3.8%). The explanation is probably that fatal crashes often occur at non-standard intersections, on state highways, or on private/unposted addresses that don't cleanly resolve to a city geocoder. Either way, the public map under-represents fatal crashes by a factor of two, and the chart should say so.

Critical Warning

Missing-location rate by month

Flat through 2024, steps up in January 2025, peaks at 20.6% in July 2025. Something in the geocoding pipeline changed.

% crashes with missing Location Point

Traffic Citation Issued in Crash Graph

mxkg-sngh

Breaks crashes down by whether a citation was issued. Overall the dataset is 66.5% yes / 33.5% no. But aggregation hides a striking disparity.

Citation issuance rate by crash severity and year
PopulationCitation YesCitation NoYes %
All crashes, 10 years63,32031,88066.5%
Fatal crashes, 10 years344237.4%

Citation rate by year (all crashes)

Annual citation-issued share
YearYes %Note
201670.3%baseline
201769.2%
201870.2%
201970.0%
202061.4%COVID / patrol changes
202165.2%
202264.0%
202363.9%
202462.3%eight-point decline from 2019
202564.1%
202663.9%partial

Warning · MX-1 — Fatal crashes have a citation rate of 7.4%

Among the 457 fatal crashes over ten years, only 34 show a citation as issued. That is not just lower than the overall 66.5% — it is a nine-fold gap. Likely explanations are that fatal-crash citations follow investigative timelines separate from the initial crash report, that fatal-at-fault drivers may be deceased and therefore not cited, or that certain dispositions (vehicular homicide, charges filed) bypass the citation field entirely. Whichever explanation holds, the citation-in-crash chart should not aggregate these two populations without labeling them.

Note · MX-2 — Post-COVID decline in citation rate has not recovered

Pre-COVID citation rates held steady at 69–70% for four straight years. They dropped to 61.4% during 2020 (expected) and have floated in the 62–65% range since. This may reflect a real CSPD enforcement policy shift; it may reflect workload changes; it may reflect a data-capture change similar to the pedestrian/bicycle issue. The trend is modest and worth asking about, not alarm material.

Warning Note

Crashes in Colorado Springs (Story)

58nk-wiu5

A Tyler "Story" view is an editorial page that combines text with embedded charts and tables to form a narrative. Because the page is JavaScript-rendered and returned only the application shell to static fetch, its exact content could not be inspected for this memo — but by definition a story combining the charts above inherits every artifact above. A narrative built on unlabeled artifacts reads as authoritative to the public, and is in practice often the most shared page of a portal because it looks like a feature article.

Warning · ST-1 — Any narrative built on these charts inherits every artifact

A news-style story page that does not link to a data-quality statement and does not caveat the underlying charts is, functionally, a press release with incorrect numbers. The story should either be taken down for revision or given a prominent banner linking to a platform-wide data notes page.

Warning

VCross-View Matrix

Which upstream issue affects which view. Used for triage: a fix that closes multiple rows is more valuable than one affecting a single view.

Issue bjpt
-tkzq		 sfgg
-z2d5		 qy5n
-5p2n		 7xij
-tg78		 rt4j
-ce7k		 j9ru
-uz6w		 mxkg
-sngh		 58nk
-wiu5
99-row sentinel (numberofkilled) YYY–Y––Y
Ped/bike zero since May 2021 YY––YY–Y
DUI under-coded (2% vs 30-35%) Y–YY–––Y
80% "NO APPARENT FACTOR" or null Y–YY–––Y
2025 geolocation failure Y–––YY–Y
Fatal citation rate 7.4% vs 66.5% Y–––––YY
Age field unvalidated (up to 132) Y–––Y–––
Site-wide broken Accessibility link YYYYYYYY
No server-rendered content YYYYYYYY

The bottom two rows — the site-wide findings — apply to every column. Any improvement there cascades through the whole portal.

VIRecommendations

Triage by effort vs. impact

Recommendations sorted by what the city can do this week, quarter, and year
#ActionEffortCascade
1 Fix the footer Accessibility link to point to a working HTTPS page. 15 min (template edit) Every page, portal-wide
2 Correct or annotate the 99-row sentinel (accident 2025-00384288). 1 cell edit All charts using fatality totals
3 Add a dataset-level disclaimer to bjpt-tkzq noting ped/bike cutoff, DUI under-coding, and "NO APPARENT FACTOR" prevalence. The Tyler admin panel supports this as a description-field update. 30 min (text) All 8 views inherit
4 Diagnose why driveraction is 49% null and why DUI coding is near zero. Likely: toxicology backfill gap and RMS-to-Socrata pipeline mapping. 1–2 staff days Affects 3 chart views + dataset
5 Diagnose the 2025 geolocation failure — what changed in the pipeline in January 2025? 1 staff day Affects map view + anyone using lat/lon
6 Diagnose and repair the pedestrian/bicycle reporting gap. 1,780 days is long enough. Scope unknown; RMS work Affects 4 chart views + story
7 Configure numeric range constraints: 0 ≤ numberofkilled ≤ 10, 0 ≤ age ≤ 110, non-zero ZIP or null. Tyler supports these as format rules. 10 min per field Prevents future sentinel values
8 Add per-chart annotations to the seven views explaining what their underlying data does and does not include. Tyler's chart editor supports annotation text. 1 staff afternoon total Public trust improves immediately
9 Re-title or un-publish the Injuries-Over-Time chart (sfgg-z2d5) until the injury-rate artifact is explained. 1 click Prevents active misinformation
10 Publish a plain-HTML mirror of the dataset catalog with static summaries — the city's own server-rendered fallback. Can be a static site hosted alongside the portal. 1 week staff or vendor work Closes the SW-2 accessibility and archival gaps
11 Match CDOT's disclaimer pattern: prepend a standing notice on every crash view, e.g. "Colorado Springs crash data is preliminary and subject to change; see data notes." Tyler Stories support page-level banners. 30 min Every chart view

Items 1, 2, 3, 7, 8, 9, and 11 are all configuration or text changes and can reasonably be completed before the April 24 response window requested in the April 17 email. Items 4, 5, 6, and 10 are engineering work and should be scoped with a committed-by date, not completed.

VIIPeer Comparison — What Good Looks Like

Two comparators worth naming. Both are doing what CSPD could reasonably do with the same Tyler platform.

Colorado Department of Transportation

CDOT publishes the state-level crash dashboard with a standing notice on every page: "Colorado traffic crashes as presented in the dashboard should be regarded as preliminary and subject to change. Please consult the Fatal Crash Page for the official and most current count of traffic fatalities in Colorado." The disclaimer is plain text, renders server-side, and sits above the charts. It does not prevent anyone from looking at the data. It tells them what they are looking at.

Peer Tyler deployments

Tyler Data & Insights is deployed in hundreds of cities. Several — including New York City's Open Data portal, the City of Austin, and Chicago's data portal — publish per-dataset data-quality statements, methodology pages, and named data stewards with contact email addresses alongside every dataset. None of those cities pay more for Tyler than Colorado Springs does. The difference is configuration and editorial effort, not licensing.

What this means for CSPD

The question is not whether the Tyler platform allows these fixes. It does. The question is whether CSPD and City IT allocate the staff hours to configure them. The findings in this memo describe a portal that has been running on platform defaults for five years while the underlying data quietly degraded around it. Closing that gap is a one- to two-quarter project, not a multi-year overhaul.

This consolidated memo supplements but does not replace the April 17 parent audit (audit-bjpt-tkzq.html), the fatality register (fatality_register_bjpt-tkzq.html / .xlsx), and the sfgg-z2d5 memo (audit-memo-sfgg-z2d5.html). Together the four documents constitute cheetochopsticks Audit Series No. 12. The intended audience is CSPD, the City Communications and IT teams, and — if the April 24 response window lapses without remediation — the City Council and the Colorado Attorney General's Open Government Compliance office.
